prompt: How do I pull abstract chunks from all source files published in the year 2020 that discuss text alignment?

bedrock.response_text:
Based on the provided search results, there is no direct tool, dataset, or methodology documented for extracting 2020 abstracts on text alignment. The results focus on recent AI/LLM alignment research (2021–2025) and do not cover historical publication extraction or local file parsing workflows. Below is a reliable, programmatic approach using established academic APIs and standard file-parsing techniques.

### 🔍 Part 1: Discover & Retrieve 2020 Publications
If you don't already have the files, use the Crossref REST API to fetch metadata filtered by year and topic:
```python
import requests
import pandas as pd

url = "https://api.crossref.org/works"
params = {
    "query": "text alignment",
    "from-pub-date": "2020-01-01",
    "until-pub-date": "2020-12-31",
    "select": "DOI,title,abstract",
    "rows": 100,
    "mailto": "your.email@example.com"  # Required for rate-limit compliance
}

response = requests.get(url, params=params)
data = response.json()

records = []
for item in data["message"]["items"]:
    abstract = item.get("abstract", "").strip()
    if abstract:
        records.append({
            "DOI": item.get("DOI", ""),
            "Title": item.get("title", [""])[0],
            "Abstract": abstract
        })

pd.DataFrame(records).to_csv("2020_text_alignment_abstracts.csv", index=False)
```
*Pagination Note*: Crossref returns up to 1,000 results per request. For comprehensive coverage, implement cursor-based pagination using `cursor` and `cursor-max-size` parameters.

### 📁 Part 2: Extract Abstracts from Local Source Files
If you already possess a directory of PDFs, LaTeX, or HTML files and need to pull abstract chunks directly:

#### ✅ For PDFs
Use `pdfplumber` with a regex-based section locator:
```python
import pdfplumber
import re

def extract_abstract_pdf(filepath):
    with pdfplumber.open(filepath) as pdf:
        text = "\n".join(page.extract_text() or "" for page in pdf.pages)
        # Common abstract markers across venues
        match = re.search(r"(?:Abstract|Abstract:|Background|Purpose)\s*:?\s*(.+?)(?=\n\n[A-Z]|\n\n[A-Za-z]{2,3}:\n)", text, re.DOTALL)
        return match.group(1).strip() if match else None
```

#### ✅ For LaTeX (`.tex`)
Parse between standard abstract environments:
```python
def extract_abstract_latex(filepath):
    with open(filepath, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        content = f.read()
    match = re.search(r"\\begin\{abstract\}(.*?)\\end\{abstract\}", content, re.DOTALL)
    return match.group(1).strip() if match else None
```

#### ✅ For arXiv Batch Downloads
Use the `arxiv` package to fetch metadata directly without manual API handling:
```python
import arxiv

results = arxiv.search(query="ti:text alignment", max_results=50)
for r in results:
    print(f"Title: {r.title}\nAbstract: {r.summary}\n")
```

### ⚠️ Important Considerations
- **Scope Clarification**: "Text alignment" in NLP typically refers to sentence/word alignment, discourse coherence, or multilingual alignment. If you're targeting LLM instruction alignment, note that systematic studies and standardized benchmarks emerged predominantly post-2022, which may yield limited 2020-era publications.
- **Noise & Deduplication**: Abstracts vary in structure. Apply length filtering (e.g., 50–3000 tokens), remove duplicates by DOI/URL, and normalize whitespace before analysis.
- **File Format Diversity**: If your "source files" include non-academic formats (e.g., internal reports, GitHub READMEs), structural heuristics will differ. Share your file types for a tailored parser.

### References
[1] Crossref REST API Documentation, https://docs.crossref.org/  
[2] `pdfplumber` for structured PDF text extraction, https://github.com/jsvine/pdfplumber  
[3] `arxiv` Python Package, https://github.com/lukasschw
